elements.sent-2411-001: For every $n\geq 4$ we construct infinitely many mutually not homotopic closed manifolds of dimension $n$ which admit a negatively curved Einstein metric but no locally symmetric metric.
elements.sent-2411-002: As a consequence of the solution to the geometrization conjecture by Perelmann, any closed manifold of dimension three which admits a negatively curved metric also admits a hyperbolic metric, and for surfaces, the corresponding statement is a classical consequence of the uniformization theorem.
elements.sent-2411-003: This statement is not true any more for closed negatively curved manifolds of dimension at least four.
elements.sent-2411-004: Indeed, Gromov and Thurston \cite{GT87 constructed for each dimension $n\geq 4$ and every $\epsilon >0$ a closed manifold $X$ of dimension $n$ which admits a metric with curvature contained in the interval $[-1-\epsilon,-1+\epsilon]$ but which does not admit a hyperbolic metric.}
elements.sent-2411-005: These manifolds will be called Gromov--Thurston manifolds in the sequel.
elements.sent-2411-006: The proof of non-existence of hyperbolic metrics on these manifolds is however indirect, that is, it is shown that among an infinite collection of candidate manifolds with pinched curvature, only finitely many admit hyperbolic metrics.
elements.sent-2411-007: Kapovich \cite{Ka07 showed that these manifolds admit convex projective structures.}
elements.sent-2411-008: Much later, Ontaneda \cite{O20 gave a very general method for constructing closed Riemannian manifolds of any dimension $n\geq 4$ with arbitrarily pinched negative curvature.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Some of the examples he found have in addition some non-zero Pontryagin numbers.
elements.sent-2411-010: It is a natural question whether these manifolds admit distinguished metrics.
elements.sent-2411-011: This was partially answered affirmatively by Fine and Promoselli \cite{FP20 who constructed negatively curved Einstein metrics on an infinite family of Gromov-Thurston manifolds in dimension four.}
elements.sent-2411-012: These metrics do not have constant curvature and therefore by the work of Besson, Courtois and Gallot \cite{BCG95 , see also the survey \cite{And10 , these manifolds are not homotopy equivalent to hyperbolic manifolds.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: The goal of this article is to extend this result to all dimensions.. We show.
elements.sent-2411-014: For any $n\geq 4$ and any $\varepsilon >0$ there exist infinitely many pairwise non-homeomorphic smooth closed manifolds $X$ of dimension $n$ with the following properties.
elements.sent-2411-015: The examples in the Theorem are Gromov Thurston manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-016: For $n\geq 5$ they seem to be the first examples of negatively curved Einstein metrics on manifolds which are not locally symmetric.
elements.sent-2411-017: Our construction builds on the ideas of Fine and Premoselli, however the examples we find in dimension four are different from the examples in \cite{FP20 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: The following question is motivated by the uniqueness of Einstein metrics on hyperbolic 4-manifolds \cite{BCG95 .}

elements.sent-2411-020: In this subsection we outline the rough strategy for the proof of \Cref{main thm and point out the difference to the proof of Fine--Premoselli.}
elements.sent-2411-021: We start by constructing a particular sequence of closed hyperbolic manifolds of which we take the branched cover.
elements.sent-2411-022: Namely, using subgroup separability in arithmetic hyperbolic manifolds of simple type, we construct for each $n \geq 4$ a sequence $(M_k)_{k \in \bbN}$ of closed hyperbolic manifolds that contain null-homologous closed totally geodesic codimension two submanifolds $\Sigma_k \subseteq M_k$ with at most two connected components and such that \begin{equation}\label{eq: big normal injectivity radius} \lim_{k \to \infty}\frac{{\rm diam}(\Sigma_k)}{R_k^\nu} = 0,\end{equation} where $R^{\nu _k$ is the normal injectivity radius of $\Sigma_k \subseteq M_k$ and, by a slight abuse of notation, ${\rm diam (\Sigma_k)$ is the maximum of the diameters of the connected components of $\Sigma_k$.}}

elements.sent-2411-032: The article is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-033: In \Cref{sec: Preliminaries we review the necessary background information.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Namely, \Cref{subsec: Einstein operator introduces the Einstein operator.}
elements.sent-2411-035: In \Cref{subsec: approximate Einstein we recall the construction of the approximate Einstein metric on branched covers due to Fine--Premoselli.}
elements.sent-2411-036: The algebraic results about arithmetic hyperbolic manifolds due to Bergeron and Bergeron--Haglund--Wise we use are contained in \Cref{subsec: subgroup separability .}
elements.sent-2411-037: These are then employed in \Cref{sec: good submanifolds to construct the sequence of closed hyperbolic manifolds containing well-behaved codimension two submanifolds.}
elements.sent-2411-038: In \Cref{subsec: Linearized Einstein is invertible we show that the linearized Einstein operator is invertible.}
elements.sent-2411-039: The existence of negatively curved Einstein metrics is then proved in \Cref{subsec: proof of main thm .}
elements.sent-2411-040: Finally, in \Cref{sec: not locally symmetric we show that we can find Gromov--Thurston manifolds in any dimension to which our construction applies and which do not admit any locally symmetric metric.. {\bf Acknowledgement: U.H.}}
